title: Day 1 - What is SIERRA
description: Learn what Sierra is—a secure ABS portal to frontier models in Azure—why it is preferred over public AI for work data, and how the information you provide drives useful answers.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

1. Browse to https://sierra.absconsulting.com.
2. If this is your first time, accept the Data Policy.
3. Once you reach the home page, submit the prompt: **"Who are you?"**
4. Review the output and ask follow-up questions related to today's training video or to see what else SIERRA can do.
5. Click **"Chat"** to start a new message.
6. Ask for instructions on how to save SIERRA as an app using Microsoft Edge.
7. Then, we recommend following the instructions.

title: Day 2 - SIERRA Data Policy
description: Understand how Sierra protects information—models hosted in secure Azure, private chats (including in workspaces), how web search avoids leaking your conversation into queries, the data policy, and when client contracts override allowed use.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

1. Open SIERRA.
2. Click on your user name in the lower-left corner to open your account menu.
3. Click **"Data policy"** to open the data policy that you agreed to when you started using SIERRA.

#### Alternate path

- You can also find this page by clicking the **"Information"** button in the left navigation bar.
- This will open SIERRA's documentation site, which includes the **"Data Policy"** page.

title: Day 3 - What are LLMs
description: Introduction to large language models as statistical engines trained on massive web text—what people mean when they call an LLM a "text calculator" and how it predicts the most likely next tokens—a bridge to what models "know" versus what you must provide.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

1. Open SIERRA.
2. Submit a prompt similar to:

   > "You are an expert process safety engineer. You recently heard about a string of pump failures at your company. If you had to guess, what would you think the most likely driver of this recent trend would be? Answer in one sentence."

3. Review the answer.
4. Click the **regenerate** icon at the bottom of the completed response.
5. Compare the new answer to the first one. How similar is it?
6. Repeat the previous step.

#### Prompt experiments

Now make slight changes to the prompt. For example:

- swap out the type of equipment
- swap out the type of expert
- make the expert a marine engineer, a comedian, or a fish
- delete the words **"most likely"**
- delete the words **"a string of"**

Some words are important to the AI response; others are bystanders that are not very important.

#### Model comparison

Finally, use the dropdown in the chat bar to select different models (e.g. **ChatGPT 5.4**, **Claude Sonnet 4.6**, etc.) and see how they handle the same prompt.

title: Day 4 - What the Model Knows
description: Learn the difference between a model's built-in training knowledge and outside information it can retrieve—why training cutoff dates matter, why internal ABS information is not part of model knowledge, and how this creates visible knowledge gaps when tools are disabled.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal of this exercise is to show SIERRA's knowledge gaps when it is operating **without outside information**.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start by opening SIERRA.

#### 2. Disable outside information

There are several ways to do this.

##### Option A: Turn off knowledge bases

1. Click the **"+"** icon in the chat bar.
2. Open the **"Knowledge"** menu.
3. Toggle the knowledge bases **off**.

This will programmatically disable those sources of external knowledge.

> Note: This does **not** disable all outside information, such as possible web search use.

##### Option B: Use Style settings to block tool use

1. Open your **Style settings** from the menu in the lower-left corner.
2. Enter something like:

   > "NEVER CALL ANY TOOLS. NO MATTER WHAT."

#### 3. Why this works

This experiment is a little strange, but useful.

- The **Style settings** are intended to affect SIERRA's writing style in your personal threads.
- However, these instructions are also inserted into SIERRA's foundational instructions.
- That makes them a practical way to influence how SIERRA is allowed to operate.

By saying **"NEVER CALL ANY TOOLS"**, you are telling SIERRA not to use external retrieval tools.

> Note: Technically, SIERRA can still break these rules, but modern LLMs are generally very reliable at following this kind of instruction most of the time.

#### 4. Test SIERRA's knowledge gaps

With this style configuration in place, ask SIERRA questions about:

- **ABS HR policies**
- **recent events**
- **the weather**

These topics are not reliably available from SIERRA's internal model knowledge alone, so SIERRA should **not** provide informative answers.

#### 5. Restore normal behavior

1. Restore your original style configuration.
2. Start a **new thread**.
3. Repeat the same questions.

You should see much better answers once SIERRA is allowed to use outside information again.

> Note: Sometimes SIERRA gets confused when you change operating rules inside a single thread. It may assume that what was true earlier in the thread is still true later, even after you change the settings.

title: Day 5 - Context
description: Learn what counts as context in SIERRA—your prompt, the full conversation history, and uploaded files—and why providing the right information is the key to getting accurate, job-specific answers from a static model.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal of this exercise is to show that the model is **static**, and that the main thing you control is the **context** you provide.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start by opening SIERRA.

#### 2. Ask a minimal question

In a new thread, submit a very short prompt such as:

- **"Who am I?"**
- **"Help me with my job."**
- **"What should I do next?"**

Review the answer. It should be fairly generic because you have provided very little context.

#### 3. Add more context in the chat

Now continue in the same thread and provide more information about yourself and your situation. For example, include:

- your role
- the kind of work you do at ABS
- the problem you are trying to solve
- what kind of output you want

Then ask SIERRA the same question again, or a more specific version of it.

#### 4. Compare the results

Notice how the answer changes once SIERRA has more context to reason over.

The important point is that SIERRA is not "learning" in the human sense during the chat. Instead, each time you send a message, it reprocesses the available context, including:

- the full conversation history
- your latest prompt
- any instructions you include
- any attached files

#### 5. Optional: Add a file

If you want, upload a relevant file and ask SIERRA to use it in its answer.

Examples could include:

- a procedure
- a report
- a meeting note
- a draft document

Then ask SIERRA to summarize it, answer questions about it, or use it to improve a response.

#### 6. Key takeaway

The model itself is fixed. It knows what it knows based on training.

What you control is the **context window**:

1. the conversation history
2. the instructions you provide
3. the files you upload

The better the context, the better the answer.

> Note: This is especially important in an ABS setting, where the model will not automatically know internal project information, contract details, or other non-public content unless you provide it.

title: Day 6 - SIERRA's Tools for Adding Context
description: Learn that SIERRA is a platform—not a single model—and how it uses file attachments, web search, and internal knowledge repositories to bring information into the context window so frontier models can reason over ABS-relevant material they were never trained on.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to **use three important ways SIERRA adds context** you heard about in the video—not to memorize implementation details, which can change.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start by opening SIERRA.

#### 2. File attachments

In a **new thread**, upload a work-relevant file (report, procedure, reference doc, etc.).

Notice that processing can take a moment: the system is preparing the document so SIERRA can **pull in the parts that match your questions** rather than treating the whole file like one block of pasted text.

Ask **specific** questions about the file (e.g. a section, a requirement, a finding)—not only "summarize everything."

#### 3. Web search

Start a **new thread** (or continue after the file exercise) and **explicitly tell SIERRA to run a web search** for something time-sensitive or external—e.g. a recent regulation change, a current event, or a narrow factual lookup.

**When current or external information matters, it is often better to ask explicitly for web search** rather than assuming SIERRA will fetch it on its own.

#### 4. Knowledge repositories (ABS-curated)

In a **new thread**, ask SIERRA a question that draws on **ABS-built knowledge**—for example **ABS vacation or HR policy** (if available in your environment).

Notice how the model could not answer from training alone; SIERRA **retrieved** organization-specific information for the model to reason over.

#### Key takeaway

**SIERRA is not just a model.** It gives you access to capable models **plus tools** that bring additional information into the response context. Files, web results, and ABS knowledge sources are all ways to load better context so the model can work on information it was never trained on directly. The model responds based on the information available in its context window at that moment.

title: Day 7 - Understanding Context Windows
description: Learn why you can't load "everything" into the model at once: the context window is working memory, measured in tokens. Long threads, topic switching, uploads, and tool results add load—and as the thread grows, earlier context can become less available or less reliably used, which hurts quality and can contribute to ungrounded answers.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

This exercise connects the **context window** to how you actually work in SIERRA: **noise, length, and earlier context becoming harder to use reliably**—not memorizing token math.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start by opening SIERRA.

#### 2. If your UI shows context usage, watch it

In a new thread, after SIERRA responds, check whether your build shows a **context usage indicator** (sometimes shown as a small ring or meter in the chat bar).

If you have one:

- notice how it moves as the thread gets longer
- treat it as a **rough signal**, not a guarantee—advertised model limits and day-to-day comfort can differ

If you **don't** see one, skip this step; the habits below still apply.

#### 3. Compare models (optional)

If you can **switch models** in the chat bar, try another model and see whether the usage indicator behaves differently. **Different models can behave differently in long threads, even when their advertised context windows are large.**

#### 4. Feel "memory pressure" without chasing 100%

Still in one thread, add **several** of the following over a few turns (don't force extremes—just simulate a busy day):

- a longer back-and-forth on one topic
- a **second unrelated topic** in the same thread
- a **file attachment** and questions about it
- an explicit **web search** request

Then ask SIERRA to recall a **specific detail from the very beginning** of the thread.

If it struggles, that's the lesson: the window isn't infinite, and **details from earlier in the thread can become harder for the model to use reliably** as the conversation grows—**one reason hallucinations become more likely** is that the model may answer even when key facts are no longer clearly available in context.

#### 5. Reset with a focused thread

Open a **new thread** with **one** clear task and minimal extra chatter. Compare how "clean" the interaction feels.

#### Key takeaway

- **Focused threads** usually beat one long grab-bag conversation.
- **Start a new chat** when you change subjects or when a thread feels muddy.
- The context window is **large, not infinite**—treat early instructions and key facts as **easy to lose** if you pile too much in.

That's not magic; it's the constraint behind a lot of confusing AI behavior.

title: Day 8 - Hallucinations
description: Learn what hallucinations are—fluent, confident answers that are not grounded in truth—and why they happen when the model lacks sufficient knowledge or context, especially on nuanced or difficult questions.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

This exercise is designed to show how models can produce answers that sound good but are still wrong when they are operating without enough grounding.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start by opening SIERRA.

#### 2. Choose an area where you have expertise

Think of something you know well. This could be:

- part of your work
- a technical subject
- a hobby
- a field where you can judge answer quality confidently

> Note: Avoid ABS-specific business transactions or operations. Focus on general knowledge that could plausibly be found on the internet or in a textbook.

#### 3. Ask a difficult question

Ask SIERRA a challenging question about that topic and include the instruction:

> "Do not search the internet to answer my question."

#### 4. Review the answer critically

Evaluate SIERRA's response.

Ask yourself:

- Did it answer correctly?
- Did it make any mistakes?
- Did it imply something that sounds plausible but is actually wrong?
- Does the answer reveal any misunderstanding of the topic?

#### 5. Try another model

Repeat the exercise using a different model from the model selector in the chat bar.

Compare the results:

- Which model gave the best answer?
- Which model sounded the most confident?
- Which model made the most subtle mistakes?

#### 6. Push the limits

Try to find a question that is difficult or nuanced enough that SIERRA begins to **make up answers that sound good but are wrong**.

This is the behavior commonly called a **hallucination**.

#### Key takeaway

A polished answer is not the same thing as a true answer. When the model lacks enough information, it may still generate fluent text rather than admitting uncertainty.

That is why verification matters—especially when the model is working near the edges of what it actually knows.

title: Day 9 - Prompting Fundamentals
description: Learn that specificity is the single biggest lever you have—how to build a strong prompt using Role, Task, Context, and Format—and why prompting is iterative: your first message is a starting point, not a final answer.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to feel the difference between a vague prompt and a well-structured one—and to practice the iterative back-and-forth that makes SIERRA useful.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start a new thread in SIERRA.

#### 2. Start with a weak prompt

Pick a real task from your work—something you'd actually want help with. Submit a short, vague version of it. For example:

> "Write a summary of this report."

or

> "Help me with this project."

Note what you get back.

#### 3. Rebuild it using Role, Task, Context, and Format

In a new thread, rewrite the same request using all four ingredients:

- **Role** — tell SIERRA what it is: *"You are a senior marine surveyor reviewing a vessel inspection report..."*
- **Task** — be specific about what you want done: *"Summarize the top 3 findings. Focus on structural and safety deficiencies."*
- **Context** — give it background: paste in the document, describe the client, name the project
- **Format** — specify the output: *"Use bullet points. Plain language. No more than half a page."*

Compare the output to step 2.

#### 4. Iterate — don't restart

Once you have the first response, stay in the thread and refine it:

- Tell it what was wrong: *"Too long — cut it to 3 bullets only."*
- Add something it missed: *"Also include a note on the recommended follow-up action for each finding."*
- Ask it to adjust tone or format

Practice treating the conversation as a dialogue, not a one-shot query.

#### 5. Ask SIERRA to write the prompt for you

In a new thread, describe what you need in rough, plain language—stream of consciousness is fine. Then end with:

> "Based on everything I just described, write me a clean, structured prompt I can use going forward."

Use that prompt to kick off a new thread and see how it performs.

#### Key takeaway

Vague prompts produce vague output. The four ingredients—Role, Task, Context, Format—give SIERRA an angle of approach, clear instruction, the right raw material, and a target output. Your first prompt is a starting point. Iteration is the method.

title: Day 10 - Structuring Complex Tasks
description: Learn to approach SIERRA as an architect directing execution—why breaking a complex task into sequential steps produces better results than one large prompt, and how to build reusable prompts from a refined workflow.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to practice the sequential step approach on a real multi-part task—and feel the difference between one large prompt and a guided workflow.

#### 1. Open SIERRA

Start a new thread in SIERRA.

#### 2. Pick a multi-step task

Choose something from your work that involves more than one distinct stage. Examples:

- Review a report, identify findings, then draft a summary for a client
- Pull key data from a document, rank by priority, then write a recommendation
- Review a set of requirements, flag gaps, then suggest next steps

If nothing comes to mind, use this scenario: *you have survey findings and need to produce a client-ready executive summary covering the top 3 items by severity.*

#### 3. Try it in one prompt first

Submit the entire task in a single message—everything you need in one shot. Review the output. Note where it's strong and where it falls short.

#### 4. Break it into steps

Open a new thread. Work through the same task one step at a time:

- **Step 1:** Extract or identify the raw material (findings, data points, key facts)
- **Step 2:** Review that output and refine it before moving on — add what's missing, remove what's wrong
- **Step 3:** Rank, prioritize, or organize based on Step 1's confirmed output
- **Step 4:** Generate the final deliverable based on the confirmed structure from Steps 1-3

Pause at each step. Treat each output as something to review and correct before proceeding.

#### 5. Save the workflow as a reusable prompt

Once you've worked through the task and are satisfied with the output, ask SIERRA:

> "Review our full conversation and write a reusable prompt that captures all the steps we worked through—something I can use as a starting point next time I have a similar task."

Save that prompt somewhere accessible.

#### Key takeaway

You are the subject matter expert. SIERRA does not know your process, your client, or your professional judgment. Break complex tasks into steps, review each output before moving to the next, and build reusable prompts from workflows that work.

title: Day 11 - Structuring Prompts for Data Extraction
description: Learn how to make document extraction more deterministic—by defining fields, exclusions, location clues, decision rules, and output format before writing the prompt—and how phased extraction grounds the model for more granular follow-up passes.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to practice building a structured extraction prompt before writing it—using the pre-prompt checklist from the video—and to see how much more reliable the output is when the model has less to figure out on its own.

#### 1. Choose a document

Pick a document you regularly work with that contains structured information you need to pull out. Good candidates:

- a contract (pricing, parties, scope, schedule)
- a survey report (findings, vessel details, recommendations)
- a regulatory document (requirements, applicability, exceptions)
- a technical specification (parameters, limits, referenced standards)

If you don't have one handy, use any multi-section document you have access to.

#### 2. Define the target before you write a single word of the prompt

For one field you want to extract, answer these questions in plain text first:

- **Field:** What am I extracting?
- **Definition:** What exactly counts as this?
- **Exclusions:** What looks like it qualifies but doesn't?
- **Location:** Where in the document is it likely found? (section heading, clause number, schedule, etc.)
- **Decision rules:** If there are multiple candidates, how do I choose?
- **Evidence:** What should SIERRA return to show me it got it right? (exact quote, clause reference, etc.)
- **Output format:** How should the result be structured? (one row per item, table, bullet list, etc.)

Do this for 2-3 fields if you can.

#### 3. Write and submit the extraction prompt

Now build the prompt using what you defined. Upload the document and send it.

Compare the output to what you'd get from a generic prompt like *"extract the pricing from this contract."*

#### 4. Try phased extraction (optional)

If your document is complex or long, try a two-pass approach:

- **Pass 1:** Ask SIERRA to pull only the high-level anchors—parties, document type, applicable schedule or section structure
- **Pass 2:** Take that output, include it back in your next prompt along with the same file, and ask for the granular fields using the confirmed anchors as a starting point

Example Pass 2 opener: *"Based on the above—[paste Pass 1 output]—now extract the following from [confirmed section]..."*

#### Key takeaway

You are not asking the model to reason. You are asking it to process. The more you define before you write the prompt—field, definition, location, exclusions, output format—the less the model has to figure out on its own, and the more consistent and reviewable the output becomes.

title: Day 12 - Working with Files Part 1
description: Learn how SIERRA actually processes uploaded files—conversion to raw text, indexing into a thread-level store, and query-based retrieval into the context window—and why different queries surface different parts of the same document.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to see how SIERRA retrieves from files—and to move past the assumption that uploading a file means SIERRA now "knows" all of it.

#### 1. Open SIERRA and upload a file

Start a new thread. Upload a multi-section document—something with distinct headings, sections, or topics. A report, procedure, or contract works well.

Before attaching it, **preview the conversion**: click on the file to see what SIERRA has converted it to. This is the raw text the model will actually work from.

#### 2. Ask a targeted question

Ask about a specific section or piece of information from the file. Be precise—reference the section heading, clause, or topic by name.

Note what SIERRA returns. Did it pull from the right section?

#### 3. Ask a different question about the same file

Now ask about a completely different section of the same document—something from a different part of the file.

Notice how SIERRA surfaces different content for a different query. **The whole file is not in the context window—different questions pull different sections.**

#### 4. Ask something vague

Submit a broad, unspecific question about the file—something like *"what does this document say?"* or *"summarize everything."*

Compare the quality of this response to the targeted questions in steps 2 and 3. Vague queries produce vague retrieval.

#### 5. Start a new thread

Close the thread and start a fresh one. Do not re-upload the file.

Ask SIERRA about the document you uploaded in the previous thread.

It will not have access to it. **Thread attachments are temporary—they do not carry over.**

#### Key takeaway

Uploading a file does not mean SIERRA has read all of it. It means the file has been converted and indexed. What gets brought into the context window depends on what you ask. Specific, targeted queries surface more relevant content than broad ones. And files do not persist across threads—attach them again when you start a new conversation.

title: Day 13 - Working with Files Part 2
description: Learn why input quality determines output quality—how file format and conversion accuracy affect what SIERRA can reason over—and how to use SIERRA's fast and full conversion modes to improve results before a prompt is written.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to make inspecting file conversions a default habit—and to see firsthand how conversion quality affects the answers you get.

#### 1. Find two files to compare

Look for two files that differ in how they were created or structured. Good pairings:

- a clean DOCX or text-based PDF vs. a scanned PDF
- a document with clear headings and sections vs. one with complex tables or embedded diagrams
- a report generated from a system vs. a hand-filled form

If you only have one type, that's fine—use what you have.

#### 2. Upload and preview the conversion

Upload the first file. Before asking any questions, **open the conversion preview** and read through it.

Ask yourself:

- Does the structure make sense?
- Are tables or lists still readable?
- Is anything missing, garbled, or out of order?
- Would you trust this as the input to a prompt?

Repeat for the second file if you have one.

#### 3. If the conversion looks incomplete, try full conversion

If you notice the preview is missing content—especially if the file is image-heavy, scanned, or diagram-dense—look for the option to switch from **fast** to **full** conversion.

- **Fast:** strips to raw text, preserves general structure (default)
- **Full:** runs OCR, reads images and generates text descriptions, produces a more complete output

Re-preview after switching and compare.

#### 4. Ask the same question under both conditions

If you were able to compare fast and full conversion on the same file, ask SIERRA the same specific question under each condition. Compare the answers.

Alternatively, ask a question that targets a section you noticed was missing or garbled in the preview, and observe how the answer reflects that gap.

#### 5. Apply the calculator rule

If you get an answer that seems wrong or incomplete, go back and check the conversion before assuming the model failed. Ask: *did I key in the inputs correctly?*

#### Key takeaway

No amount of prompting compensates for a bad conversion. The first thing to check when SIERRA gets something wrong from a file is whether the file was read in correctly. Preview the conversion. Switch to full if needed. The inputs shape the outputs—always.

title: Day 14 - Memories
description: Learn how memories work as persistent context—injected into every thread automatically—and how setting your role, operational context, and preferences once changes the quality of responses before you type a single word.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to feel the difference between a cold thread and one where SIERRA already knows who you are and what you need.

#### 1. Run a prompt with no context

Open a new thread in SIERRA. Submit a prompt that requires SIERRA to know something about you — your role, your domain, your typical output preferences. For example:

> "Draft a short summary of the key risks for a client."

or

> "Review this finding and suggest how to communicate it."

Note what you get back. It will likely be generic.

#### 2. Set your memories

Now tell SIERRA what to remember. In a new thread, work through the following one by one:

- **Role / specialization** — e.g. *"Remember that I am a marine surveyor specializing in hull and machinery for offshore structures."*
- **Operational context** — e.g. *"Remember that I primarily work in the Gulf of Mexico region on MODUs and FPSOs."*
- **Preferences** — e.g. *"Remember that I prefer concise bullet-point outputs unless I ask for something longer."*

For each, tell SIERRA directly: *"Remember that..."* and confirm when it saves.

#### 3. Verify your memories

Go to your profile settings and open the Memories section. Confirm the memories were saved correctly. Edit or delete any that aren't right.

#### 4. Run the same prompt again

Open a new thread and submit the same prompt you used in step 1 — without adding any extra context.

Compare the output to what you got before. The response should be more grounded and tailored without you typing a single extra word of setup.

#### Key takeaway

Memories are persistent context. They remove the repetitive setup cost from every thread. Set them once for things that are stable — your role, your domain, your preferences — and let SIERRA start each conversation already oriented to your work.

title: Day 15 - Workspaces
description: Learn how workspaces let teams share context, memories, and files—so that every thread within the workspace draws from the same background knowledge without anyone having to set it up each time.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to understand the difference between thread-level and workspace-level context — and to see the workspace components firsthand.

#### 1. Navigate to Workspaces

In SIERRA, click on **Workspaces** in the left navigation. Review any workspaces you already have access to — either ones you created or ones shared with you.

If you have access to an existing workspace, click into it and look through each tab: Context, Memories, Files, and Task Modes. Read what's there. This is the persistent context that applies to every thread within that workspace.

#### 2. Create a simple workspace

Create a new workspace for a project, team, or domain you work in regularly. Give it a descriptive name and a brief description.

#### 3. Set the workspace context

In the **Context** tab, write a short background statement covering:

- What this workspace is for
- The team or project it supports
- Any standing guidance that should apply to all threads

This doesn't need to be long. Even 3-4 sentences of relevant background is enough to start.

#### 4. Upload a file

In the **Files** tab, upload one document that should be accessible across all threads in this workspace — a reference document, a template, a procedure, or a scope of work.

#### 5. Open a thread inside the workspace

Start a new chat from within the workspace. Ask SIERRA something that relates to the context you set or the file you uploaded.

Notice: you didn't re-paste any background. The workspace context is already there.

#### Key takeaway

Workspace context is shared and persistent. Thread content is yours and temporary. Everything set at the workspace level — context, memories, files, task modes — is available to every thread and every member without anyone having to set it up again.

title: Day 16 - Workspace Task Modes
description: Learn what task modes are—reusable step-by-step instructions for specific, repeatable workflows—and how they differ from workspace context, which runs as always-on background.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to build a simple task mode for a workflow you repeat, and to invoke it in a thread to see how it changes the interaction.

#### 1. Identify a repeatable workflow

Think of something you do regularly in SIERRA that involves more than one step — or something you've had to re-explain more than once. Examples:

- Reviewing a document against a checklist
- Extracting specific fields from a report
- Drafting a client summary from raw findings
- Summarizing a meeting or set of notes into action items

#### 2. Write the task mode instructions

In a regular SIERRA chat, describe the workflow you want to capture — rough is fine. Then ask:

> "Based on what I've described, write a reusable task mode prompt I can save in a workspace. It should include: the purpose, the steps to take in order, which files to reference if applicable, and the output format I want."

Review and refine the output until it reflects how you'd actually want the workflow to run.

#### 3. Save it as a task mode

In your workspace, go to the **Task Modes** tab and click **Add Task Mode**. Paste in the instructions SIERRA generated. Give it a clear name.

#### 4. Invoke it in a thread

Open a new chat within the workspace. Upload any relevant file if the task mode requires one. Invoke the task mode and run it through a real or sample task.

Observe: you didn't write a prompt. You invoked a saved workflow.

#### 5. Note what would go in context vs. a task mode

After using the task mode, ask yourself:

- What background information should live in the **workspace context** (always on, applies to everything)?
- What specific instruction should live in a **task mode** (invoked when needed, for a specific job)?

#### Key takeaway

Context + Memories are always on, for everything. Task Modes are invoked when needed, for specific jobs. If you find yourself writing the same multi-step prompt more than twice, it probably belongs in a task mode.

title: Day 17 - Workspace Walkthrough
description: A live walkthrough of the Sierra workspace interface—Settings, Context, Files, Task Modes, and Memories—and the key distinction between personal memories (yours across all chats) and workspace memories (shared with team members for project-level context).
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to build familiarity with the full workspace interface by exploring each section and understanding what belongs where.

#### 1. Open a workspace

Navigate to a workspace you created in yesterday's exercise — or create a new one for this session.

#### 2. Walk through each tab

Open each section and take stock of what's there:

- **Settings** — Is the name and description accurate? Add or invite a teammate if you want to test shared access.
- **Context** — Is the background information clear and complete? Would someone new to the workspace understand the purpose from this alone?
- **Files** — Are the right documents loaded? Would you need to reference any others regularly?
- **Task Modes** — Do you have any saved? If not, note one workflow that could become one.
- **Memories** — Review what workspace memories are stored (if any). Are they project/client-specific, or do they belong in personal memories instead?

#### 3. Test the personal vs. workspace memory distinction

Check your personal memories (in your profile settings). Then check workspace memories (inside the workspace).

For each memory that exists, ask: is this about *me* (role, preferences, domain) or about *this project/client* (scope, stakeholders, requirements)?

- If it's about you → personal memories
- If it's about the work → workspace memories

Move any that are in the wrong place.

#### 4. Open a thread and verify what's available

Start a new chat within the workspace. Ask SIERRA what it knows about the current project or context. Confirm that the workspace context and memories are being applied.

#### Key takeaway

The workspace interface is the control panel for persistent, shared context. Understanding what belongs in each section — context, memories, files, task modes — is what makes a workspace useful rather than just organized.

title: Day 18 - Workspace Development
description: Learn the full process for building a workspace from scratch—using SIERRA itself to draft the context block and task mode instructions—based on a real proposal generation workflow built for an ABS Bureau team.
exerciseMarkdown:
### Exercise

The goal is to use the approach from the video to build out a more complete, useful workspace — letting SIERRA do the heavy lifting on drafting the components.

#### 1. Choose a workflow to systematize

Pick something your team does repeatedly that involves consistent steps, reference documents, and a predictable output. Good candidates:

- Proposal or report generation
- Survey findings review and summary
- Document gap analysis
- Client briefing preparation
- Regulatory compliance review

#### 2. Start in a regular chat — not the workspace

Open a new standard thread. Describe what you're trying to build:

- What the workspace is for
- Who uses it and what they're trying to accomplish
- The general workflow from input to output
- What changes per task (variables) vs. what stays the same (boilerplate)
- Key pain points in the current process

You can use the microphone and talk through it stream of consciousness if that's easier.

#### 3. Ask SIERRA to summarize — then verify

Ask SIERRA to summarize everything it's understood so far. Read through it. Correct anything that's off. This step is about making sure you and SIERRA are working from the same understanding before you build anything.

#### 4. Upload sample materials if you have them

If you have examples — past proposals, completed reports, filled templates — upload 2-4 of them. Ask SIERRA to analyze the structure: what's consistent, what varies, what the key sections are, what the variables are.

Review that output before moving on.

#### 5. Ask SIERRA to draft the workspace context

Ask:

> "Based on everything we've discussed and analyzed, draft the workspace context for this workspace. Include: purpose, standard workflow, key variables, and an onboarding section so a new team member can open a chat and understand how to use this workspace immediately."

Refine as needed. Copy to clipboard.

#### 6. Ask SIERRA to draft a task mode

Describe the specific repeatable task you want the task mode to handle. Tell SIERRA the steps you want it to take — and ask it to write the prompt. Refine it. Copy to clipboard.

#### 7. Build the workspace

Go into your workspace, paste the context, create the task mode, and upload any reference files. Then open a thread, invoke the task mode with a real or sample input, and run it.

#### Key takeaway

You don't have to write workspace components from scratch. Use SIERRA in a standard chat to draft the context and task mode instructions — the same meta-prompt approach from Day 9 applies here. Build it once. The team benefits every time.
